content.text: Pull requests (PRs) — or merge requests on GitLab — are proposals to integrate one branch into another. They bundle code review, discussion, CI checks, and approval before agent changes reach main, making them the primary quality gate for prompt and tool modifications.
What it is
You push a branch to the remote and open a PR targeting
main
. Reviewers comment on diffs; CI runs tests and agent evals; after approval the platform merges. PRs create an audit trail: who approved a tool permission change and when.

git switch -c fix/search-tool-timeout
# change tools/search.py timeout 30 -> 10
git commit -am "Reduce search tool timeout to prevent hung agent loops"
git push -u origin fix/search-tool-timeout
gh pr create --title "Fix search tool timeout" --body "Fixes #142. Eval latency p95 -12%."
Why it matters

Agent systems fail in subtle ways — a prompt edit increases hallucination rate. PR review plus automated eval diffs catch regressions before production. PRs also satisfy change-management requirements in enterprise deployments.
Benefits
Code review
— second pair of eyes on tool schemas and safety
CI integration
— lint, unit tests, golden-set evals on every PR
Discussion
— inline comments on specific prompt lines
Documentation
— PR description links issue, metrics, rollout plan
Approval rules
— require security review for new tools
Trade-offs
Latency
— small fixes wait for review cycles
Rubber stamping
— approvals without reading diffs defeat the purpose
Large PRs
— prompt + refactor + new tools in one PR are hard to review
Fork workflow overhead
— open-source contributions need maintainer triage
How it works
Effective PR description
Include: problem statement, approach, eval metrics (before/after), rollout/rollback plan, screenshots of agent UI if relevant, linked issue IDs.
Review checklist for agent changes
Prompt changes: unintended behavior shift? eval diff attached?
New tools: input validation, auth, rate limits, idempotency?
Config: secrets not in diff? defaults safe?
Dependencies: license and supply-chain acceptable?
Draft PRs
Open draft PR early for work-in-progress feedback on agent architecture — mark ready for review when evals pass.
When to use it
Always
for changes to main in team agent repos
Solo projects
— optional but still useful for CI history and self-review
Skip PR
only for automated bot commits (dependency bumps with auto-merge)
Real-world example

PR adds email-sending tool. Security reviewer checks sandbox and confirmation step. CI runs 200 golden eval cases — pass rate 98.5% vs 98.4% baseline. Two approvals; squash merge. Deployment workflow triggers on main with canary 5% traffic.
Common mistakes
PR too large
— split prompt change from unrelated refactor
No CI status
— merging red builds ships broken agents
Missing eval evidence
— "seems fine" is not sufficient for prompt merges
Force-push after review started
— invalidates review context without notice
Best practices
Keep PRs small and focused; stack PRs if needed
Attach eval summary tables or links to experiment runs
Request specific reviewers (security for tools, ML for prompts)
Use CODEOWNERS for prompts/ and tools/ directories
Squash merge for clean main history; preserve detail in PR body
Security & reliability
Require signed commits or org membership for merge
Block merge if secret scanning fails
Require branch up-to-date with main before merge
Mental model
A PR is a formal change request with evidence attached — like submitting a revised agent playbook for sign-off before it goes live on the floor.
Key takeaways
PRs = propose, review, CI, approve, merge
Primary gate for prompt, tool, and orchestration changes
Include eval metrics and rollback plan in description
Small PRs, meaningful reviews, no merge on red CI
CODEOWNERS and approval rules for high-risk agent capabilities
